topic: Müller, K., & Zhang, L. (2023). Quantifying confabulation in autoregressive language models: The confabulation coefficient and cascade effects. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, 78(4), 1123–1145. doi:10.48550/arXiv.2305.12345
final_review:
The concept of confabulation in autoregressive language models has gained significant attention in recent years, with researchers exploring its implications and potential applications (Müller & Zhang, 2023). One of the primary concerns with large language models is their tendency to produce hallucinations, which can be defined as the generation of text that is not based on any actual input or context. This phenomenon has been extensively studied, with some researchers proposing methods for detecting hallucinations (Rawte et al., 2023; Sui et al., 2024).

The study of confabulation and hallucinations in language models has also been informed by research in cognitive science and neuroscience. For example, studies on the ventromedial prefrontal cortex have shown that lesions in this region can produce early functional alterations during remote memory retrieval (Gilboa et al., 2009). Similarly, research on anosognosia has highlighted the importance of awareness and self-perception in cognitive processes (Vocat et al., 2010). These findings have implications for the development of large language models, particularly in healthcare applications where accuracy and reliability are crucial (Bradshaw et al., 2025; Tam et al., 2024).

The use of large language models in healthcare has been explored in various studies, with some researchers proposing frameworks for human evaluation of these models (Tam et al., 2024). Others have demonstrated the potential of large language models in medical imaging, highlighting their ability to generate easy-to-understand reports (Salam et al., 2024). However, the application of large language models in healthcare also raises concerns about the potential for hallucinations and confabulation, which can have serious consequences in clinical settings (Rawte et al., 2023).

In addition to healthcare, large language models have been explored in other domains, including education and physics (Gregorcic, 2023). Researchers have also investigated the use of large language models in time-series forecasting (Zhou et al., 2021) and computer vision (Everingham et al., 2014). The development of large language models has also been influenced by research in machine learning and deep learning (Janiesch et al., 2021; Müller et al., 2022).

Despite the progress made in understanding confabulation and hallucinations in large language models, there are still significant research gaps that need to be addressed. For example, the development of more effective methods for detecting and mitigating hallucinations is an area that requires further research. Additionally, the application of large language models in healthcare and other domains raises important questions about the potential risks and benefits of these technologies (Bradshaw et al., 2025; Tam et al., 2024).

In conclusion, the study of confabulation and hallucinations in large language models is a complex and multifaceted field that draws on research from cognitive science, neuroscience, and computer science. While significant progress has been made in understanding these phenomena, there are still important research gaps that need to be addressed. Further research is needed to develop more effective methods for detecting and mitigating hallucinations, as well as to explore the potential applications and risks of large language models in various domains.
